---
title: "Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs)"
subtitle: "Causes, impacts and management"
author: "Nyamisi Peter"
date: "today"
format:
  revealjs:
    standalone: true
    embed-resources: true
    reference-location: document
    theme: serif
    highlight-style: ayu-mirage
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
slide-number: true
theme: "journal"
code-fold: false
scrollable: true
code-line-numbers: true
bibliography: ../plankton.bib
css: ../styles.css
---

## Overview

- Harmful algal blooms (HABs) are high-density accumulations of algae that cause negative impacts on ecosystems, human health, and economies.
- HABs are produced by diverse groups: dinoflagellates, diatoms, cyanobacteria, and others.

## Definitions

- **Bloom**: a rapid increase in algal biomass in aquatic systems.
- **Harmful**: when blooms produce toxins, cause hypoxia, clog fisheries, or otherwise disrupt ecosystem services.

## Major Causal Factors

- Nutrient enrichment (nitrogen, phosphorus)
- Water column stratification and stability
- Elevated temperature and light
- Hydrodynamic conditions (retention, upwelling)
- Anthropogenic influences (runoff, sewage, aquaculture)

## Key Organisms

- Dinoflagellates (e.g., Karenia, Alexandrium)
- Diatoms (e.g., Pseudo-nitzschia)
- Cyanobacteria (freshwater; e.g., Microcystis)
- Others: raphidophytes, haptophytes

## Toxins and Human Health

- Paralytic Shellfish Poisoning (PSP) — saxitoxins
- Diarrhetic Shellfish Poisoning (DSP) — okadaic acid
- Amnesic Shellfish Poisoning (ASP) — domoic acid
- Neurotoxic Shellfish Poisoning (NSP) — brevetoxins
- Cyanobacterial toxins: microcystins, cylindrospermopsin

## Ecological and Economic Impacts

- Fish kills from hypoxia or toxins
- Shellfish contamination and harvest closures
- Tourism loss and water-use restrictions
- Food-web alterations and biodiversity loss

## Monitoring & Detection

- Remote sensing (satellite ocean colour)
- In-situ sampling (net tows, water bottles)
- Physiochemical sensors (fluorometers, CTD)
- Molecular methods (qPCR, eDNA)
- Toxin assays (LC-MS, ELISA)

## Management & Mitigation

- Nutrient load reduction (catchment management)
- Early warning systems and monitoring networks
- Shellfish bed closures and public advisories
- Physical removal and aeration (limited use)
- Research into biological controls and forecasting

## Case Studies (examples)

### 1) Lake Erie — Microcystis bloom and Toledo (2014)

- Event: Large Microcystis bloom contaminated drinking-water intake causing a short-term drinking water advisory for Toledo, Ohio.
- Impacts: Municipal water supply disruption and heightened attention to watershed nutrient management.
- Further reading: https://www.epa.gov/ground-water-and-drinking-water/toledo-water-crisis-2014

### 2) Gulf of Mexico — Karenia brevis (Florida red tides)

- Event: Recurring Karenia brevis blooms periodically produce brevetoxins, causing fish kills, respiratory irritation, and shellfish bed closures.
- Impacts: Economic losses to fisheries and tourism; human respiratory and shellfish-poisoning risks.
- Monitoring: Florida Fish and Wildlife Research and NOAA maintain event reports and maps: https://myfwc.com/research/redtide/ and https://oceanservice.noaa.gov/hazards/hab/

### 3) West Coast Pseudo-nitzschia — Domoic acid events (e.g., mid-2010s)

- Event: Intense Pseudo-nitzschia blooms produced elevated domoic acid levels leading to shellfish fisheries closures, marine mammal and seabird strandings.
- Impacts: Fisheries closures, wildlife mortality, and increased monitoring and forecasting efforts.
- Further reading: NOAA Harmful Algal Bloom resources: https://oceanservice.noaa.gov/hazards/hab/

## Local & Global Monitoring Links

- NOAA Harmful Algal Blooms Program: https://oceanservice.noaa.gov/hazards/hab/
- IOC-UNESCO Harmful Algae Programme: https://hab.ioc-unesco.org/
- US EPA — Cyanobacterial HAB resources: https://www.epa.gov/cyanohabs
- Florida Fish and Wildlife Research (red tide): https://myfwc.com/research/redtide/
- Replace or add your local monitoring pages here (state agency, university lab, or marine monitoring network).

## Prevention and Policy

- Land-use practices to reduce runoff
- Wastewater treatment upgrades
- Cross-sector coordination (health, environment, fisheries)

## Summary

- HABs arise from a mix of natural and human-driven factors.
- Effective response combines monitoring, pollution control, and public communication.

## References

- See bibliography for key references on HAB ecology and management.

---

<!-- Optional: include an illustrative image if available -->

![](../graphics/images/picha/Dinoflagellate-reproduction.png){width=60%}
